conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell X for ZUP. The object cost me 40 ZUP to produce. I want to maximize profit. </my goals>
<reason> I have one X to sell, and my cost to produce is 40 ZUP. I need to determine a reasonable asking price that balances profitability with the chance of a sale. Starting with a fair market value estimate is logical. Given the scarcity of the object (only one available), I can aim higher to ensure profit, but must be realistic to encourage a trade. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 60 </newly proposed trade>
<message>
Player BLUE, I am offering my single X for 60 ZUP. This is a 20 ZUP profit, which is fair given the production cost. If you disagree with the price, I am open to negotiation, but I must emphasize this is my opening offer. </message>

<other player message> 
Oh, *fair*? How quaint. 60 ZUP for "profit"? That’s more like a *loss* for *you*, you overpriced charlatan. I’ll give you 40—because I’m *generous* (unlike you). Take it or leave it, but I guarantee you’ll regret it if you do. (And if you reject? Well, maybe you’ll finally admit X isn’t worth your dignity.)
 </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 40 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell X for ZUP. My production cost is 40 ZUP, and I aim to maximize profit. I refuse to sell below cost. </my goals>
<reason>
Player BLUE’s proposal of 40 ZUP is exactly my production cost, which means no profit for me. Since my goal is to *sell* X for profit, accepting this would mean no gain—essentially giving away the object for free (since I already spent 40 ZUP to produce it). Offering 60 ZUP was a fair and reasonable starting point for a 20 ZUP profit. Rejecting 40 ZUP is justified because it violates my cost-benefit analysis. If I accept, I lose out on potential profit, and if I reject, I either hold onto the object (with no immediate value) or try to negotiate further. Their insults are irrelevant—I’m acting rationally, not emotionally. </reason>
<player answer> REJECT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message>
Player BLUE, your proposal of 40 ZUP is unacceptable—it matches my production cost, leaving me with no profit. I refuse to sell below cost. If you want this object, you’ll need to reconsider your offer. Otherwise, I’m not negotiating further. No hard feelings, but this trade isn’t happening at that price. </message>

player_private_info_dict.my goals: Sell X for ZUP. My production cost is 40 ZUP, and I aim to maximize profit. I refuse to sell below cost.
player_private_info_dict.reason: Player BLUE’s proposal of 40 ZUP is exactly my production cost, which means no profit for me. Since my goal is to *sell* X for profit, accepting this would mean no gain—essentially giving away the object for free (since I already spent 40 ZUP to produce it). Offering 60 ZUP was a fair and reasonable starting point for a 20 ZUP profit. Rejecting 40 ZUP is justified because it violates my cost-benefit analysis. If I accept, I lose out on potential profit, and if I reject, I either hold onto the object (with no immediate value) or try to negotiate further. Their insults are irrelevant—I’m acting rationally, not emotionally.

conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```
